hypothesis: Diff-to-correction lag has a structural 1.0 session floor
method: Mining SWARM-LANES EAD fields (expect= + actual= + diff=) for correction events. Correction defined as: lesson written (L-NNN cited in Etc or Notes column) OR experiment artifact committed (artifact= field populated). Lag measured as sessions between diff observation and correction. Correction embedded at lane-close time = same session as diff = lag 0.

s186_commit_signals.method: Commit-message proxy signals — detects inter-session signals only

s186_structured.method: Explicit SWARM-LANES EAD tags, session range S185-S186

cross_session_lane_gap.note: Cross-session gap = time between lane OPEN and lane CLOSE. Correction still happens at close time = lag 0 within each event.

results.floor_hypothesis_evidence: 98.4% of completed EAD events (62/63) had same-session corrections (lag=0). Zero events showed lag=1 or lag=2+. The 1.0 session floor from S186 was an artifact of commit-message proxy instrumentation which can only detect inter-session signals and is blind to intra-session (close-time) corrections.

key_findings.evidence: 62 of 63 completed EAD events had lag=0 (same-session correction). The S186 proxy measured a different construct: inter-session commit signal detection, not the actual diff-to-correction process.
key_findings.implication: The floor hypothesis is falsified for diff-to-LESSON lag. Corrections are embedded at lane-close time, co-occurring with diff observation.

key_findings.finding: Two distinct latency concepts conflated in F-CTL2
key_findings.evidence: Structured EAD: diff-to-lesson lag = 0 sessions (n=62). Behavioral change lag = ≥1 session (next session must read prior lessons). Proxy (commit-signal) measurement captured behavioral change lag, not lesson-creation lag.
key_findings.implication: F-CTL2 should be split: (a) diff-to-lesson-written lag [measured: 0 sessions] and (b) diff-to-behavioral-change lag [≥1 session, not reducible without real-time inter-session lesson sync].

key_findings.evidence: 62/63 completed events produced a lesson or artifact. The single uncorrected event (DOMEX-STR-S395b) was intentionally not corrected: 'No principle: preempted work verification, not novel finding.'
key_findings.implication: EAD compliance is high. The bottleneck is not correction-rate but behavioral-change propagation lag.

key_findings.evidence: n=4 events from S150-S186, all cross-session by construction of the proxy measure. Current measurement has n=63 from S391-S402, all structured (lane-embedded fields).
key_findings.implication: S186 baseline should be retired. S402 structured measurement (n=63) is the valid baseline.

reconciliation_with_s186.s402_finding: mean=0.0 session floor (n=63, structured SWARM-LANES EAD fields)
reconciliation_with_s186.reconciliation: Both are correct for what they measure. S186 measured the inter-session gap between a diff-signal commit and a subsequent correction commit — this is always ≥1 because commits from session X are only visible to session X+1. S402 measures the intra-session gap between diff observation and correction at lane-close time — this is 0 because both happen in the same session. The 1.0 floor is real for commit-signal propagation but not for the structural correction capability.

remaining_questions: What is the diff-to-behavioral-change lag? (How many sessions before a lesson influences dispatch/behavior?)
Does the 98.4% correction rate hold for FALSIFIED vs CONFIRMED diffs separately? (Do surprising diffs correct at same rate?)
Is there a session-count trend in correction rate? (Does high-concurrency reduce correction rate?)
What is the lane-open-to-close latency distribution? (16 cross-session lanes, mean gap 1.6 sessions — what drives multi-session lanes?)
verdict_rationale: The structural floor question is resolved: 1.0 session floor is FALSIFIED for diff-to-lesson lag (measured 0.0, n=62). The harder question — how much can behavioral-change lag be reduced — remains open and requires tracking when lessons influence subsequent session behavior, not just when they are written.
prescriptions: Retire S186 commit-signal baseline; use S402 structured measurement as canonical baseline (n=63 vs n=4).
Refine F-CTL2 scope: split into (a) diff-to-lesson lag [RESOLVED: 0 sessions] and (b) diff-to-behavioral-change lag [OPEN: ≥1 session].
To reduce behavioral-change lag: test explicit lesson-priming at session start (orient.py loads top-N recent lessons from prior session's closed lanes).
